titleStmt.title: Changes in cycling economy and fractional utilization of VȮ 2peak during a 40-min maximal effort exercise test with acute hypobaric hypoxia corresponding to 2800 m of altitude

analytic.title: Changes in cycling economy and fractional utilization of VȮ 2peak during a 40-min maximal effort exercise test with acute hypobaric hypoxia corresponding to 2800 m of altitude

biblStruct.note: Received: 29 June 2023 | Revised: 16 September 2023 | Accepted: 25 September 2023

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents
application.label: b64991a

startPage=-1, endPage=-1, consolidateCitations=0, consolidateHeader=0, consolidateFunders=0, includeRawAffiliations=false, includeRawCitations=true, includeRawCopyrights=false, generateTeiIds=false, generateTeiCoordinates=[], flavor=null

div.p: Introduction: Peak oxygen uptake (VȮ 2peak ) declines by ~7% per 1000 m of increasing altitude, whereas exercise performance seems reduced to a lesser extent.

For example, 800-10 000 m track and field performances are decreased by 0.4%-2.4% above 1000 m as compared to below and some studies show similar drops in cycling performance. A greater decline in VȮ 2peak than in endurance performance with altitude suggests a higher fractional utilization of VȮ 2peak (%VȮ 2peak ).
Therefore, we hypothesized that the %VȮ 2peak is higher with acute hypoxic exposure than near sea level.

Methods: Sixteen lowlanders (8 women, age: 31 ± 7 years [mean ± SD], body mass: 68 ± 8 kg, VȮ 2peak : 60 ± 8 mL min -1 kg -1 ) underwent cycling testing in a hypobaric hypoxic chamber on 6 test days, three conducted at 300 m and three at 2800 m of acute altitude. At both altitudes, VȮ 2peak was determined, and during a 40-min all out maximal effort time trial (TT), mean power output (MPO) and mean VȮ 2 (%VȮ 2peak ) were assessed.

Results: VȮ 2peak decreased by 11.2 ± 3.0% (p < 0.001), while MPO during the TT declined by 10.7 ± 3.1% (p < 0.001) at 2800 m as compared to 300 m. During the TT, %VȮ 2peak was higher at altitude, corresponding to 75.9 ± 4.5% at 300 m and 78.8 ± 4.2% at 2800 m (p = 0.011), and cycling economy (mL O 2 kJ -1 ) was poorer (+3.4 ± 2.7%, p < 0.001).

div.head: Conclusion:
div.p: The %VȮ 2peak was higher during a cycling TT at 2800 m of altitude than near sea level, while cycling economy was poorer. This resulted in a similar reduction in performance and VȮ 2peak . Future studies should address the physiological mechanisms underlying the elevated %VȮ 2peak .

div.head: K E Y W O R D S
div.p: altitude, fractional utilization of VȮ 2peak , hypoxia, performance, time trial

div.head: Note:
div.p: The training volume corresponds to self-reported hours of strength and endurance training per week or total cycling hours per week, respectively. Mean ± SD.

div.head: | INTRODUCTION
div.p: Endurance performance is determined by a combination of factors, including the peak oxygen uptake (VȮ 2peak ), the fractional utilization of VȮ 2peak (%VȮ 2peak ), the anaerobic capacity, and the exercise economy, referring to the proportion of the generated energy that converts into mechanical work. 1,2 The %VȮ 2peak is the VȮ 2 sustained over a given time as a percentage of VȮ 2peak . 2 Contrary to the oxygen uptake at lactate threshold, it is a dynamic parameter that depends on the distance or duration of the endurance event. For instance, the %VȮ 2peak of elite runners corresponds to 80%-85% during a marathon, while it can reach more than 90% during a 10 km race. 3,4 Also, different environmental conditions alter the average %VȮ 2peak during an endurance event. 5 Most endurance competitions are held at low altitudes, but especially long-distance running and cycling events could involve several ascents to higher altitude. To which extent high altitude affects the %VȮ 2peak is currently unknown.

Acute exposure to a hypoxic environment in lowlanders results in a lowered arterial O 2 tension and compromises O 2 delivery to the exercising muscle. Consequently, as altitude increases, endurance performance and VȮ 2peak decline. 6 In moderately trained runners, VȮ 2peak decreases from 300 m to 2800 m of altitude, corresponding to a 6.3% reduction on average per 1000 m of increasing altitude. 7 However, the decline in VȮ 2peak and performance with altitude seems of different magnitude. For example, middle-and long-distance track and field competition performance is reduced to a lesser extent than VȮ 2peak . Depending on the event distance, the decline ranges from an average 0.4%-4.1% in events taking place above 1000 m as compared to below 1000 m of altitude. 8,9 Similarly, the reduction in cycling performance seems smaller with acute hypoxia as compared to the decrease in VȮ 2peak . 10,11 The latter, however, varies considerably and is influenced by different variables, 12,13 such as the participants' training status, and the duration and type of performance test (e.g., time trial vs. time to exhaustion and laboratory vs. field test). 6 At natural altitude up to 3200-3500 m, the reduced air density reduces aerodynamic drag which more than compensates for the drop in VȮ 2peak with hypoxia. 14 In the laboratory setting, the seemingly disproportionate reduction in VȮ 2peak and performance with hypoxia suggests that the %VȮ 2peak is augmented. Furthermore, it was observed that cycling economy, assessed as O 2 cost of constant load exercise, as well as anaerobic capacity is similar at sea level and at altitude. 11,[15][16][17] Hence, the aim of this study was to test the hypothesis that the average %VȮ 2peak during a cycling time trial (TT) is higher with acute hypoxia than near sea level.

div.head: | METHODS
div.p: The study was approved by the Ethics Committee of the Norwegian School of Sport Sciences (ref. 151-270820). Oral and written informed consents were obtained from all participants prior to the start of the experiments and the study was carried out in accordance with the Declaration of Helsinki.

div.head: | PARTICIPANTS
div.p: Sixteen endurance-trained lowlanders (8 women) were recruited for this study (Table 1). Inclusion criteria were age between 18 and 45 years for women and 18 and 40 years for men and pursuit of systematic cycling training including indoor cycling. Exclusion criteria were smoking, hypertension, and cardiovascular or metabolic disease.

div.head: | STUDY DESIGN
div.p: Participants reported to the laboratory on six occasions and completed three different test protocols twice, once

Women Men Overall N 8 8 16 Age (years) 29 ± 9 33 ± 3 31 ± 7 Body mass (kg) 63 ± 6 73 ± 5 68 ± 8 Height (m) 1.68 ± 0.05 1.79 ± 0.05 1.74 ± 0.07 VȮ 2peak (mL min -1 kg -1 ) 53.5 ± 5.3 65.5 ± 5.3 59.5 ± 8.0 Total training volume (h week -1 ) 12.6 ± 6.0 12.6 ± 3.3 12.6 ± 4.8 Cycling training volume (h week -1 ) 8.2 ± 3.2 9.8 ± 2.5 9.0 ± 3.0 | 3 of 10 OBERHOLZER et al. performed in normoxia (300 m) and once in hypoxia (2800 m), in a randomized order. On test Days 1 and 2, participants completed Protocol 1, which was a submaximal constant load test to determine cycling economy followed by a VȮ 2peak familiarization test. On test Days 3 and 4, a blood lactate profile test, a VȮ 2peak test and a 20 min maximal effort TT familiarization were conducted (Protocol 2), whereas on test Days 5 and 6, the 40-min TT was performed (Protocol 3). At least 24 h passed between test Days 1-3 and 48 h between test Days 4-6. All tests were performed in a hypobaric chamber (Norwegian Universal Technology AS) set to either 300 m (976 ± 2 hPa) or 2800 m (724 ± 2 hPa), at 17.9 ± 0.3°C, with approximately 1 ms -1 headwind and a relative humidity between 50% and 80%. O 2 was continuously supplied to the closed chamber to keep partial pressure of O 2 at 20.90 ± 0.025%. This was achieved by injecting O 2 at a rate equal to the sum of the O 2 uptake by the participant and test leader (feed-forward) and finetuning by a feedback loop. CO 2 kept <2500 ppm with the help of CO 2 scrubbers. The participants were blinded toward the simulated altitude and all chamber operation procedures were standardized to reduce the chance that the participants can figure out the altitude. The participants were asked to guess the altitude following each test day.

div.head: | EXERCISE TESTS
div.p: Participants were instructed to plan the study days as high-intensity sessions and prepare for each test day accordingly. During the tests, participants could drink water ad libitum, but were not allowed to ingest any food or sports drinks. All performance tests were conducted using the same electromagnetically braked cycle ergometer (Excalibur Sport; Lode BV). The seat height, handlebar position, and the horizontal distance between seat and bottom bracket were saved and kept constant for each participant throughout all tests. Pulmonary gas exchange was measured using a metabolic cart with a mixing chamber (Oxycon Pro; Jaeger). As soon as the test altitude was reached and ambient conditions were stable, the gas analyzer and flow transducer (Triple V; Jaeger) were calibrated according to the instruction manual. In the following, VȮ 2 is presented in STPD and V̇E in BTPS with the test altitude (300 or 2800 m) used for barometric pressure. Heart rate (HR) was assessed using a sports watch and a chest belt (Polar RS800; Polar Electro Oy). Rate of perceived exertion (RPE) was determined using the BORG scale from 6 to 20, arterial oxygen saturation by pulse oximetry placed on the right index finger (SpO 2 ; 901-M; Masimo) and capillary blood lactate concentration ([La]) from a fingertip with a biochemical analyzer (Biosen C-line; EKF Diagnostic).

div.head: | Cycling economy
div.p: First, the participants rested seated on the bicycle for 5 min. Thereafter, they cycled at 125 W (n = 6) or 175 W (n = 10) for 10 min and were instructed to maintain a cadence of 80 RPM. VȮ 2 was measured continuously throughout the test and the average VȮ 2 during the last 5 min of cycling was used for analysis of cycling economy.

div.head: | Blood lactate profile
div.p: The start load was chosen based on the participant's cycling level with the goal that at least four incremental stages can be completed below what was defined as lactate threshold. After cycling 5 min at the initial stage, the load was increased by 25 W every 5 min. Capillary blood was obtained from a fingertip at the end of each stage and analyzed for [La]. VȮ 2 and HR were measured continuously between Minutes 2 and 4.5 of each stage and the average was used for analysis. The power at lactate threshold was defined as the power at which [La] was ≥2.1 mmol L -1 higher than the [La] measured at the first stage, 18 calculated from the exponential relationship of [La] and power.
The test was terminated when [La] was exceeding the [La] at lactate threshold. The VȮ 2 and HR at lactate threshold were calculated accordingly.

div.head: | Peak oxygen uptake (VȮ 2peak )
div.p: Following the blood lactate profile test, the participants cycled at a self-selected cool-down load for 10 min. Subsequently, an incremental test was conducted to determine VȮ 2peak and peak power (W max ). The start workload was set to 100 W for women and 150 W for men and increased by 25 W min -1 until voluntary exhaustion. The participants were explained that the test was terminated when the pedaling frequency fell below 60 RPM. The highest VȮ 2 of the 30 s moving averages was used to determine VȮ 2peak . W max was calculated as W max = W compl + 25 W•(t/60), with W compl being the last completed workload and t the number of seconds spent on the uncompleted workload. Maximal HR, RPE, and SpO 2 were recorded at exhaustion. On test Days 1 and 2 (Protocol 1), a VȮ 2peak familiarization was conducted in 12 of the 16 participants to acquaint them with the equipment, the test environment and the effort. The highest VȮ 2peak obtained either during Protocol 1 or 2 was used for analysis. Maximal aerobic power (MAP) was determined as the workload at VȮ 2peak by extrapolation from the linear relationship between power and VȮ 2 as obtained from the blood lactate profile test.

div.head: | 40-Min maximal effort time trial (TT)
div.p: The participants warmed up at a self-selected load for 10 min. Subsequently, the TT started at a load set to 60% of sea level MAP (181 ± 30 W) for 5 min at both altitudes. Thereafter, the power control unit was handed to the participants who were instructed to increase or decrease the power output with the goal of achieving the highest possible average power output for the remaining 35 min. During the test, everyone was verbally motivated to perform at their maximal effort.

The participants could see the completed time and received oral feedback on the power output change, but were blinded toward all the other measured variables, including the absolute power output. HR, SpO 2 , [La] and RPE were assessed every 10 min and immediately after TT completion. VȮ 2 was measured continuously throughout the TT except during Minutes 11-12, 21-22, and 31-32 when the individuals could remove the mouthpiece and drink water. Therefore, the participants were instructed to maintain the power output between Minutes 10-12, 20-22, and 30-32 and the missing VȮ 2 from the drinking period was extrapolated from the minute prior to the mouthpiece removal. Cycling economy during the TT was calculated as VȮ 2 per unit of work (kJ) and was averaged over 60 s, as was VȮ 2 , minute ventilation (V̇E) and power output. To practice the controlling of the power output and the pacing of such effort at both altitudes, all individuals underwent a 20-min TT familiarization on Days 3 and 4 (Protocol 2). 6 | MEASUREMENT ACCURACY Since the inspired fraction of O 2 (FiO 2 ) and CO 2 (FiCO 2 ) in the closed chamber can deviate from the respective values at gas calibration, the measurement accuracy of the automated indirect calorimetry system was verified by sampling Douglas bags. Two bags were sampled during the VȮ 2peak test (3 min into the test and close to exhaustion) and three bags during the TT (3, 23, and 33 min into the test) in six participants. Expired air was collected via a mouthpiece into 100 L Douglas bags (Hans Rudolph Inc.) and the collecting time for each bag was automatically measured by a watch (Excelsior). The volume (Flow turbine, Type S-430; Kl Engineering) and expired fraction of O 2 and CO 2 (oxygen analyzer type S-3A/1 and carbon dioxide analyzer type CD-3A; Ametek Inc.) were measured for each bag outside of the hypobaric chamber. The FiO 2 and FiCO 2 were used from the chamber readings, which were also checked against a Douglas bag collected from the ambient air in the hypobaric chamber. The VȮ 2 and VĊO 2 were calculated for STPD and V̇E for BTPS conditions with the test altitude (300 or 2800 m) used for the barometric pressure. The accuracy of the flow turbine was verified with a 3.00-L motorized calibration syringe (Vacumed 17056, Vacumetrics). The O 2 and CO 2 analyzers were calibrated with dry room air (20.93% O 2 ; 0.04% CO 2 ) and linearity was verified with a calibration gas (15% O 2 ; 6% CO 2 ).

div.head: | STATISTICAL ANALYSIS
div.p: Statistical analysis was performed, and figures were made using R version 3.6.1 and R studio version 2022.02.0. [19][20][21][22][23][24][25][26] Data were evaluated for normality and equal variance by graphical inspection of normal Q-Q and residual plots. A paired t-test was used to compare the differences in exercise performance between 300 m and 2800 m. If data were skewed, a Wilcoxon signed rank test was applied instead.

For statistical analysis of the TT data, the full 40-min data set was used. A secondary analysis was performed to investigate sex differences in response to hypoxia by applying an independent t-test or Mann-Whitney U-test, respectively. Also, differences in VȮ 2 between the indirect calorimetry system and the Douglas bags were evaluated by independent t-test or Mann-Whitney U-test, respectively. Two-way repeated measures ANOVA was applied to evaluate the effects of TT duration (time) and altitude, and their interaction, on variables assessed during the TT.

To examine the development of HR, SpO 2 , RPE, and [La] during the TT, pairwise comparisons with Tukey p-value adjustment were performed between different time intervals. The relationship between the percentage decline in VȮ 2peak with altitude and the SpO 2 at VȮ 2peak at 2800 m was assessed using Spearman's rank correlation.

div.head: | Blinding
div.p: On the first study day, 67% of the individuals did not know the altitude at which the experiments were conducted, whereas 20% guessed the altitude correctly and 13% guessed wrongly. On study Days 2-6, 0%-12% (min-max) did not know, 69%-87% guessed correctly, and 7%-31% guessed wrongly, indicating a learning effect to detect the correct altitude after the first study day. 2). The average %VȮ 2peak during the TT was higher at altitude, corresponding to 75.9 ± 4.5% at 300 m and 78.8 ± 4.2% at 2800 m (p = 0.011). Despite the lower power output at altitude, average V̇E was 16.3 ± 10.7% higher (p < 0.001) at 2800 m than at 300 m. The average cadence during the TT was lower at altitude than near sea level, with 86 ± 7 RPM and 90 ± 7 RPM (p < 0.001), respectively. HR gradually increased during the TT (time: p < 0.001) and increased more at 300 m than at 2800 m (time*altitude: p < 0.001), which was also reflected in RPE (time*altitude: p < 0.05) (Figure 3). Similarly, [La] increased during the TT (time: p < 0.001), but was consistently higher at 2800 m than at 300 m (altitude: p < 0.001, time*altitude: p > 0.05). Note that the first measurement was obtained 10 min into the TT. As expected, the mean SpO 2 during the TT was higher near sea level than at altitude (altitude: p < 0.001), on average 94.5 ± 1.2% at 300 m and 82.7 ± 2.6% at 2800 m.

div.head: | Cycling economy
div.p: During submaximal constant load cycling, there was no difference in VȮ 2 between altitudes (0.3 ± 4.4%, p = 0.982).

In contrast, the cycling economy during the TT, calculated as average VȮ 2 (mL)/kJ, was 3.4 ± 2.7% poorer at 2800 m than at 300 m (altitude: p < 0.001; Figure 4), with a deterioration in economy over time until 30 min into the TT (time: p < 0.001).

div.head: | Sex differences
div.p: There were no differences between women and men in the relative response to 2800 m in any exercise variables, except for a different change in RER max at VȮ 2peak with altitude in women (+4.8 ± 10.4% from 300 m to 2800 m) than in men (-6.0 ± 7.3%, p = 0.050).

div.head: | Measurement accuracy of pulmonary gas exchange
div.p: The VȮ 2 assessed by the automated system was 9.7 ± 5.4% (p < 0.001) higher at the start and 12.4 ± 4.7% (p < 0.001) higher towards the end of the VȮ 2peak test than the VȮ 2 by Douglas bag. The difference between the two systems was consistent between 300 m and 2800 m, being 10.7 ± 4.6%

T A B L E 2 Exercise test outcomes. 300 m 2800 m p-value VȮ 2peak test VȮ 2peak (mL min -1 ) 4149 ± 791 3685 ± 724 <0.001 VȮ 2peak (mL min -1 kg -1 ) 60.6 ± 7.9 53.8 ± 7.5 <0.001 W max (W) 349 ± 49 313 ± 44 <0.001 V̇E max (L min -1 ) 166 ± 29 176 ± 29 0.012 RER max 1.12 ± 0.11 1.07 ± 0.10 0.632 RPE max 19.2 ± 1.0 18.9 ± 1.1 0.203 HR max (bpm) 182 ± 8 178 ± 12 0.006 SpO 2min (%) 93 ± 3 82 ± 4 <0.001 [La] max (mmol.l -1 ) 10.0 ± 1.9 11.3 ± 1.9 0.009 Lactate threshold [La] (mmol.l -1 ) 3.2 ± 0.3 3.6 ± 0.5 0.007 Power output (W) 233 ± 43 193 ± 36 <0.001 HR (bpm) 156 ± 9 154 ± 10 0.083 VȮ 2 (ml.min -1 ) 3246 ± 650 2697 ± 542 <0.001 %VȮ 2peak 78.8 ± 5.2 73.9 ± 6.9 0.009 40 min time trial Average power output (W) 223 ± 39 199 ± 34 <0.001 Accumulated work (kJ) 536 ± 94 477 ± 82 <0.001 Accumulated VȮ 2 (L) 126 ± 23 116 ± 20 <0.001 Average VȮ 2 (mL min -1 ) 3141 ± 567 2890 ± 503 <0.001 Average V̇E (L min -1 ) 96 ± 17 112 ± 24 <0.001 Average %VȮ 2peak 75.9 ± 4.5 78.8 ± 4.2 0.011 Average cycling economy (mL O 2 kJ -1 ) 235 ± 11 243 ± 10 <0.001 Note: Mean ± SD. Abbreviations: HR, Heart rate; [La], blood lactate concentration; RER, respiratory exchange ratio; RPE, rate of perceived exertion; SpO 2 , arterial oxygen saturation by pulse oximetry; V̇E, minute ventilation; VȮ 2 , oxygen uptake; VȮ 2peak , peak oxygen consumption; %VȮ 2peak , fractional utilization of VȮ 2peak ; W max , maximal power output.

div.head: | DISCUSSION
div.p: In this study, the %VȮ 2peak during a cycling TT was increased with acute exposure to a simulated altitude of 2800 m as compared to near sea level. However, contrary to our hypothesis, the capacity to utilize a higher fraction of VȮ 2peak in hypoxia did not convert into a proportionally higher power output but was rather compensating for the poorer cycling economy during such effort at altitude. As hypothesized, the %VȮ 2peak during a 40-min maximal TT effort was higher with acute hypoxia than near sea level. This would imply that the VȮ 2peak decrement with altitude is more pronounced than the decrement in TT performance, provided that anaerobic capacity and cycling economy are preserved. However, the increase in %VȮ 2peak is smaller than assumed based on the reported decline in VȮ 2peak 6,7 and performance with altitude, 10,11,27 even if the decline in performance varies between studies. 12,13 In this regard, numerous studies highlight that exercise economy remains unaltered in response to hypoxic exposure. 6,15 In accordance, we found similar VȮ 2 during submaximal constant load cycling at acute simulated altitude and near sea level. However, during the 40-min TT, cycling economy was poorer in hypoxia, as represented by the increased VȮ 2 /kJ. A possible reason could be the higher work of breathing during maximal effort, reflected by the increased V̇E during the TT in hypoxia. Furthermore, the more pronounced fatigue as a result of the higher relative intensity (higher %VȮ 2peak ) in the beginning of the TT at altitude may have affected cycling economy later (Figure 4). In this regard, running economy correlates negatively with relative intensity at intensities above 70% VȮ 2peak in that VȮ 2 /km gradually increases with increasing relative intensity. 28 Taken together, we found higher whole-body VȮ 2 for a given work during a maximal effort TT in acute hypoxia. Whether VȮ 2 is also elevated in the isolated m. quadriceps during all-out exercise at altitude remains to be determined.
Hence, studies investigating the mechanisms underlying the higher %VȮ 2peak with hypoxia by invasive measurements of a-vO 2 differences across the m. quadriceps are warranted.

We observed a decline in VȮ 2peak of 11% from 300 m to 2800 m. This is lower than in previous studies in which the reduction in VȮ 2peak averaged 7.7% for every 1000 m of ascent. 7 One reason may be the lower aerobic fitness level of the participants in the present study (VȮ 2peak : 59.5 ± 8.0 mL min -1 kg -1 ), as the slope of decline in VȮ-2peak with altitude is steeper in highly endurance-trained individuals. [29][30][31] Also, previous predictions for the decrement in VȮ 2peak with altitude were mostly based on data in men and 50% of the participants in the present study were women. Due to a lower sea level VȮ 2peak , women may have a lower decline in VȮ 2peak . Nevertheless, we and others 6 did not find any sex differences in the relative exercise responses to altitude. Importantly, we tested the accuracy of the automated indirect calorimetry system in our hypobaric chamber and sampled Douglas bags during the VȮ-2peak test. The relative difference between the two systems was consistent between 300 m and 2800 m, supporting that differences in VȮ 2 between 300 m and 2800 m are valid. Furthermore, we performed a VȮ 2peak familiarization trial to ensure that the individuals practiced executing the test to their maximal effort. As previously observed, 7 the relative decline in VȮ 2peak correlated negatively with SpO 2min at 2800 m, with individuals desaturating more (SpO 2 of 76%-77%), showing a larger fall in VȮ 2peak . Hence, the reason for the lower decrement in VȮ 2peak with altitude is not fully clear, as we have both a robust sample size and accurate measurements of VȮ 2peak .

The mean power output during the 40-min TT was 11% lower with acute hypoxic exposure than near sea level. Alterations in cycling performance with altitude are challenging to compare among studies, as the outcome depends on the sports discipline, test duration, test condition (laboratory or field setting, time to exhaustion or TT), participants' fitness level and acclimatization status. 6 All participants performed a TT familiarization in hypoxia and near sea level to ensure they were accustomed to its pacing in a laboratory environment. Furthermore, from the RPE during the TT at 300 m and 2800 m, it is evident that the athletes were exhausted upon completion. Thus, from our findings, it emerges that the decline in VȮ 2peak and 40-min cycling TT performance are similar in magnitude with acute exposure to 2800 m of altitude.

div.head: | Methodological considerations
div.p: First, the TT started with a load corresponding to 60% of sea level MAP for 5 min at both altitudes. This set the same absolute baseline conditions for the TT at 300 m and 2800 m but resulted in a higher relative intensity at altitude during the first 5 min. As mentioned above, the latter may have negatively affected the cycling economy. However, apart from the onset of the TT, the participants were continuously encouraged to perform maximally and to adjust the power output if necessary. Accordingly, the RPE was similar at both altitudes, but showed a more accentuated increase in the TT at 300 m. This indicates that the different relative intensity at the start of the TT did not prevent the individuals from performing maximally during the subsequent 35 min. In support of that, it was previously observed that a conservative start into a 10 km TT was compensated for by an increase in intensity in the final sprint, while with a fast start the cyclists failed to increase the intensity accordingly towards the end of the TT. 32 Second, the elevated %VȮ 2peak is based on VȮ 2peak measured on a separate day in a non-fatigued state and VȮ 2peak may decline during the TT. 33 While there is no reason to argue that this small progressive decrement in VȮ 2peak would be any different with hypoxic exposure, there is no evidence to confirm this.

F I G U R E 2 Cardiopulmonary responses and power output during the time trial at 300 m and 2800 m, specifically oxygen uptake (VȮ 2, A), power output (B), fractional utilization of VȮ 2peak (%VȮ 2peak , C), and minute ventilation (V̇E, D). Lines represent the mean and the shaded area the standard deviation. Effect of altitude (A), effect of time (T), interaction of altitude, and time (AxT). Data were averaged over 60 s and is only displayed from Minute 2 when pulmonary gas exchange was stabilized (in panel A, C, D).

div.head: | Conclusion
div.p: In conclusion, the %VȮ 2peak was higher during a 40-min all-out TT with acute exposure to hypobaric hypoxia simulating 2800 m altitude as compared to 300 m. However, cycling economy was also poorer resulting in a similar reduction in TT performance and VȮ 2peak . Future studies should delve more into the potential pulmonary and muscular mechanism underlying the increased %VȮ 2peak and the poorer exercise economy during maximal performance with acute hypoxia.

div.head: | Perspectives
div.p: The effect of acute altitude on VȮ 2peak and exercise economy have been investigated extensively, showing a robust decline in VȮ 2peak , 6 while economy is thought to be unaffected by moderate hypoxia. 11,15,16 Also, there is no indication that anaerobic capacity is affected by hypoxia. 17 However, the fourth major factor in the performance equation, the %VȮ 2peak , 1,2 is investigated to a lesser extent, neither in normoxia nor in hypoxia. Furthermore, the decline in performance with altitude is poorly described, especially during exercise of longer duration. To understand the reduction in performance with altitude, these factors should be investigated collectively. Altitude could be a useful perturbation to unravel the factors that determine %VȮ 2peak . For instance, the relationship between physiological thresholds and %VȮ 2peak needs further investigation, as the current results indicate that lactate threshold may occur at a lower %VȮ 2peak in hypoxia than near sea level. Further, the adaptation in %VȮ 2peak with altitude acclimatization is worthwhile exploring, as well as the underlying relationship between the changes in performance and VȮ 2peak with acclimatization.

figure.figDesc: at 300 m and 11.5 ± 5.9% at 2800 m (p = 0.940). The difference in cycling economy (O 2 kJ -1 ) between the automated system and the Douglas system was similar at 300 m and 2800 m at all time points during the TT (3 min: 6.7 ± 3.2% vs. 7.4 ± 7.4% [p = 0.826], 23 min: 11.7 ± 3.3% vs. 9.4 ± 3.5% [p = 0.273], 33 min: 10.5 ± 2.4 vs. 8.8 ± 4.4% [p = 0.400]).

figure.head: F I G U R E 1
figure.figDesc: Correlation between the SpO 2min at VȮ 2peak at 2800 m and the percentage decline in VȮ 2peak from 300 m to 2800 m. R indicates the Spearman's rank correlation coefficient and p the p-value. Arterial oxygen saturation by pulse oximetry (SpO 2 ), peak oxygen uptake (VȮ 2peak ).

figure.head: F I G U R E 3
figure.figDesc: Physiological responses during the time trial at 300 m and 2800 m, specifically heart rate (A), rate of perceived exertion by Borg scale (B), blood lactate concentration (C), and arterial oxygen saturation by pulse oximetry (SpO 2 , D). Bold lines represent the mean with standard deviation, thin lines depict individual data. Effect of altitude (A), effect of time (T), interaction of altitude and time (AxT). *p < 0.05 versus 10 min, #p < 0.05 versus 20 min, £p < 0.05 versus 30 min.

figure.head: Table 2 )
figure.figDesc: . This percentage decline in VȮ 2peak correlated with the SpO 2 at VȮ 2peak at 2800 m (p = -0.716, p = 0.003; Figure1). At VȮ 2peak , RER and RPE were similar at both altitudes, whereas, V̇E and [La] were higher (p < 0.010), and HR and SpO 2 were lower (p < 0.010) at 2800 m than at 300 m. The MAP declined by 10.0 ± 6.3%, corresponding to 303 ± 53 W at 300 m and 272 ± 46 W at 2800 m (p < 0.001).

row.cell: Accumulated VȮ 2 and total work declined by 7.8 ± 4.5%
The power output and VȮ 2 at lactate threshold decreased
with the lactate threshold being at a higher %VȮ 2peak at

note.p: 8.2 | VȮ 2peak and blood lactate profile VȮ 2peak and W max decreased from 300 m to 2800 m, by 11.2 ± 3.0% and 10.2 ± 3.7% (p < 0.001), respectively 16000838, 2024, 1, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/sms.14511 by <Shibboleth> member@tudublin.ie, Wiley Online Library on [10/08/2024]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License

16000838, 2024, 1, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/sms.14511 by <Shibboleth> member@tudublin.ie, Wiley Online Library on [10/08/2024]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License

div.head: FUNDING INFORMATION
div.p: This study was funded by the Norwegian School of Sport Sciences. Laura Oberholzer is supported by the Swiss National Science Foundation (grant P2SKP3 194956).

div.head: DATA AVAILABILITY STATEMENT
div.p: The data that support the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.

div.head: ACKNO WLE DGE MENTS
div.p: The authors thank all participants for their commitment and time investment in this study. Many thanks are also extended to Svein Leirstein, Magne Lund-Hansen, Espen Spro, Øyvind Skattebo, and Olav Vikmoen for their expert technical assistance.

div.head: CONFLICT OF INTEREST STATEMENT
div.p: The authors do not have any conflicts of interest. The results of the study are presented clearly, honestly, and without fabrication, falsification, or inappropriate data manipulation.

analytic.title: Endurance exercise performance: the physiology of champions

biblStruct.note: Joyner MJ, Coyle EF. Endurance exercise performance: the physiology of champions. J Physiol. 2008;586(1):35-44.

analytic.title: The Kenyan runners

biblStruct.note: Larsen HB, Sheel AW. The Kenyan runners. Scand J Med Sci Sports. 2015;25:110-118.

analytic.title: Limiting factors for maximum oxygen uptake and determinants of endurance performance

biblStruct.note: Bassett DR Jr, Howley ET. Limiting factors for maximum oxy- gen uptake and determinants of endurance performance. Med Sci Sports Exerc. 2000;32(1):70-84.

analytic.title: Training and bioenergetic characteristics in elite male and female Kenyan runners

biblStruct.note: Billat V, Lepretre PM, Heugas AM, Laurence MH, Salim D, Koralsztein JP. Training and bioenergetic characteristics in elite male and female Kenyan runners. Med Sci Sports Exerc. 2003;35(2):297-304.

analytic.title: Performance and pacing during cycle exercise in hyperthermic and hypoxic conditions

biblStruct.note: Périard JD, Racinais S. Performance and pacing during cycle ex- ercise in hyperthermic and hypoxic conditions. Med Sci Sports Exerc. 2016;48(5):845-853.

analytic.title: Maximal and submaximal exercise performance at altitude

biblStruct.note: Fulco CS, Rock PB, Cymerman C. Maximal and submaximal exercise performance at altitude. Aviat Space Environ Med. 1998;69:793-801.

analytic.title: Linear decrease in VO2max and performance with increasing altitude in endurance athletes

biblStruct.note: Wehrlin JP, Hallén J. Linear decrease in VO2max and perfor- mance with increasing altitude in endurance athletes. Eur J Appl Physiol. 2006;96(4):404-412.

analytic.title: Environmental and venue-related factors affecting the performance of elite male track athletes

biblStruct.note: Hollings SC, Hopkins WG, Hume PA. Environmental and venue-related factors affecting the performance of elite male track athletes. Eur J Sport Sci. 2012;12(3):201-206.

analytic.title: Effects of altitude on performance of elite track-and-field athletes

biblStruct.note: Hamlin MJ, Hopkins WG, Hollings SC. Effects of altitude on performance of elite track-and-field athletes. Int J Sports Physiol Perform. 2015;10(7):881-887.

analytic.title: Effects of periodic carbohydrate ingestion on endurance and cognitive performances during a 40-km cycling time-trial under normobaric hypoxia in well-trained triathletes

biblStruct.note: Liao YH, Mündel T, Yang YT, Wei CC, Tsai SC. Effects of periodic carbohydrate ingestion on endurance and cognitive performances during a 40-km cycling time-trial under normobaric hypoxia in well-trained triathletes. J Sports Sci. 2019;37(16):1805-1815.

analytic.title: Acute beetroot juice supplementation does not improve cycling performance in normoxia or moderate hypoxia

monogr.title: Int J Sport Nutr Exerc Metab

biblStruct.note: MacLeod KE, Nugent SF, Barr SI, Koehle MS, Sporer BC, MacInnis MJ. Acute beetroot juice supplementation does not improve cycling performance in normoxia or moderate hy- poxia. Int J Sport Nutr Exerc Metab. 2015;25(4):359-366.

analytic.title: Combining heat stress and moderate hypoxia reduces cycling time to exhaustion without modifying neuromuscular fatigue characteristics

biblStruct.note: Girard O, Racinais S. Combining heat stress and moderate hypoxia reduces cycling time to exhaustion without modify- ing neuromuscular fatigue characteristics. Eur J Appl Physiol. 2014;114(7):1521-1532.

analytic.title: The influence of a mild thermal challenge and severe hypoxia on exercise performance and serum BDNF

biblStruct.note: Van Cutsem J, Pattyn N, Vissenaeken D, et al. The influ- ence of a mild thermal challenge and severe hypoxia on ex- ercise performance and serum BDNF. Eur J Appl Physiol. 2015;115(10):2135-2148.

analytic.title: The effect of altitude on cycling performance: a challenge to traditional concepts

biblStruct.note: Hahn AG, Gore CJ. The effect of altitude on cycling per- formance: a challenge to traditional concepts. Sports Med. 2001;31(7):533-557.

analytic.title: Exercise economy does not change after acclimatization to moderate to very high altitude

biblStruct.note: Lundby C, Calbet JAL, Sander M, et al. Exercise economy does not change after acclimatization to moderate to very high alti- tude. Scand J Med Sci Sports. 2007;17(3):281-291.

analytic.title: The effect of acute simulated moderate altitude on power, performance and pacing strategies in well-trained cyclists

biblStruct.note: Clark SA, Bourdon PC, Schmidt W, et al. The effect of acute simulated moderate altitude on power, performance and pacing strategies in well-trained cyclists. Eur J Appl Physiol. 2007;102(1):45-55.

analytic.title: Effects of acute moderate hypoxia on anaerobic capacity in endurance-trained runners

biblStruct.note: Friedmann B, Frese F, Menold E, Bärtsch P. Effects of acute moderate hypoxia on anaerobic capacity in endurance-trained runners. Eur J Appl Physiol. 2007;101(1):67-73.

analytic.title: Sex differences in performance-matched marathon runners

biblStruct.note: Helgerud J, Ingjer F, Strømme SB. Sex differences in performance-matched marathon runners. Eur J Appl Physiol Occup Physiol. 1990;61(5-6):433-439.

analytic.title: Cycling economy during the time trial at 300 m and 2800 m, specifically O 2 in mL/kJ. Lines represent the mean and the shaded area the standard deviation

persName.surname: F I G U R E 4

monogr.title: Effect of altitude (A), effect of time (T), interaction of altitude and time (AxT)

biblStruct.note: Data were averaged over 60 s and is only displayed from Minute 2 when pulmonary gas exchange was stabilized

F I G U R E 4 Cycling economy during the time trial at 300 m and 2800 m, specifically O 2 in mL/kJ. Lines represent the mean and the shaded area the standard deviation. Effect of altitude (A), effect of time (T), interaction of altitude and time (AxT). Data were averaged over 60 s and is only displayed from Minute 2 when pulmonary gas exchange was stabilized.

analytic.title: Self-paced exercise in hot and cool conditions is associated with the maintenance of %VȮ2peak within a narrow range
